summary: This is a short news-style post covering two unrelated topics. First, Joel highlights Eric Meyer's S5 slide show system built entirely on web standards (XHTML, CSS, JavaScript) as a free alternative to PowerPoint, noting that a CityDesk template makes it even easier to use. Second, he describes an intermittent IIS 6.0 bug he struggled with, which turned out to be a COM+ application identity issue where the IWAM account needed to be changed. The post is characteristic of Joel's blog style of mixing product promotion with technical problem-solving.
key_points: S5 is a standards-based (XHTML/CSS/JavaScript) slide show format that serves as a free, web-friendly alternative to PowerPoint
A CityDesk template for S5 removes the need to know HTML, making standards-compliant presentations accessible to non-technical users
An intermittent IIS 6.0 bug was traced to the COM+ application identity being set to the IWAM_Machinename account instead of the correct user
Joel enlisted his community of readers to help debug the IIS issue, thanking nearly 30 contributors by name
money_quotes: S5 is a slide show format based entirely on XHTML, CSS, and JavaScript. With one file, you can run a complete slide show and have a printer-friendly version as well.
The markup used for the slides is very simple, highly semantic, and completely accessible.
This is a pretty cool free, standards-compliant improvement on PowerPoint that lets you put your presentations on the web.
I am completely at wit's end debugging this strange (apparent) IIS bug.
key_insight: Web standards (XHTML, CSS, JavaScript) can replace proprietary tools like PowerPoint for presentations, while community collaboration remains invaluable for debugging obscure server issues.
